title: Notable Event Prioritization Model (MLTK)
app: Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit (MLTK), Splunk Enterprise Security (ES)
spl:
index=notable
| eval label=case(status="Closed - True Positive", 1, status="Closed - False Positive", 0, true(), null())
| where isnotnull(label)
| lookup asset_lookup dest OUTPUT priority as asset_priority, bunit
| eval hour=strftime(_time, "%H"), dow=strftime(_time, "%w")
| join type=left rule_name
    [search index=notable | stats avg(urgency_score) as avg_urgency, stdev(urgency_score) as std_urgency by rule_name]
| eval features=mvappend(urgency, asset_priority, hour, dow, rule_name)
| fit GradientBoostingClassifier label from urgency, asset_priority, hour, dow into notable_priority_model
| where 'predicted(label)' = 1
| sort -probability
| table _time, rule_name, dest, user, urgency, probability, asset_priority
description: SOC analysts face hundreds of notables daily. A machine learning ranking model that predicts which notables are most likely true positives — based on historical disposition, asset criticality, risk score, time-of-day, and correlated events — lets the queue self-sort so analysts investigate the highest-value alerts first.
value: SOC analysts face hundreds of notables daily. A machine learning ranking model that predicts which notables are most likely true positives — based on historical disposition, asset criticality, risk score, time-of-day, and correlated events — lets the queue self-sort so analysts investigate the highest-value alerts first.
implementation: Collect at least 6 months of closed notables with consistent dispositions from SOC analysts. Build feature vectors including: rule name, urgency, asset priority, business unit, time features (hour, day-of-week), correlated risk event count, and historical true-positive rate for that rule. Train a GradientBoostingClassifier in MLTK. Deploy as a saved search that enriches the notable queue with a `priority_score` field. SOC leads review the model's feature importance report to identify poorly-performing correlation searches. Retrain monthly and track precision/recall. Consider A/B testing the model against the default urgency ranking.
visualization: Table (prioritized notable queue), Bar chart (feature importance), Line chart (model precision over time), Single value (daily TP prediction accuracy).
knownFalsePositives: Prioritization models inherit analyst bias in historical labels — the queue can favor past habits over true severity until retrained.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit (MLTK), Splunk Enterprise Security (ES).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=notable`, `index=risk`, ES asset and identity lookups.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Collect at least 6 months of closed notables with consistent dispositions from SOC analysts. Build feature vectors including: rule name, urgency, asset priority, business unit, time features (hour, day-of-week), correlated risk event count, and historical true-positive rate for that rule. Train a GradientBoostingClassifier in MLTK. Deploy as a saved search that enriches the notable queue with a `priority_score` field. SOC leads review the model's feature importance report to identify poorly-perf…

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=notable
| eval label=case(status="Closed - True Positive", 1, status="Closed - False Positive", 0, true(), null())
| where isnotnull(label)
| lookup asset_lookup dest OUTPUT priority as asset_priority, bunit
| eval hour=strftime(_time, "%H"), dow=strftime(_time, "%w")
| join type=left rule_name
    [search index=notable | stats avg(urgency_score) as avg_urgency, stdev(urgency_score) as std_urgency by rule_name]
| eval features=mvappend(urgency, asset_priority, hour, dow, rule_name)
| fit GradientBoostingClassifier label from urgency, asset_priority, hour, dow into notable_priority_model
| where 'predicted(label)' = 1
| sort -probability
| table _time, rule_name, dest, user, urgency, probability, asset_priority
```

Understanding this SPL

**Notable Event Prioritization Model (MLTK)** — SOC analysts face hundreds of notables daily. A machine learning ranking model that predicts which notables are most likely true positives — based on historical disposition, asset criticality, risk score, time-of-day, and correlated events — lets the queue self-sort so analysts investigate the highest-value alerts first.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=notable`, `index=risk`, ES asset and identity lookups. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit (MLTK), Splunk Enterprise Security (ES). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: notable.

**Detection type** for this use case: notable — interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=notable. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **label** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(label)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **hour** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **features** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Pipeline stage (see **Notable Event Prioritization Model (MLTK)**): fit GradientBoostingClassifier label from urgency, asset_priority, hour, dow into notable_priority_model
• Filters the current rows with `where 'predicted(label)' = 1` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
• Pipeline stage (see **Notable Event Prioritization Model (MLTK)**): table _time, rule_name, dest, user, urgency, probability, asset_priority


Step 3 — Validate
Compare model scores to **analyst dispositions in Incident Review** and the training export you used, and re-run a small batch through **the MLTK experiment** to verify lift has not regressed after retraining.



Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (prioritized notable queue), Bar chart (feature importance), Line chart (model precision over time), Single value (daily TP prediction accuracy).
grandmaExplanation: We use models to order notables by true urgency so the team works the right ticket next when everything seems loud.
